epigraph.text: For his invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made.

content.text:
Humans are pattern-seekers. We notice that the sun rises each morning, 
that dropped objects fall downward, that triangles seem to have the same 
angle sum. From particular observations, we form general conclusions. 
This process is called **inductive reasoning**.

content.title: Inductive Reasoning
content.content:
**Inductive reasoning** is the process 
of observing patterns in specific examples and forming a general 
conclusion called a **conjecture**.

content.title: Finding patterns in numbers
content.problem: Find the next three numbers in the sequence: $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, \ldots$
content.solution:
We observe that these are perfect squares: $1^2, 2^2, 3^2, 4^2, 5^2, \ldots$

The pattern continues: $6^2 = 36$, $7^2 = 49$, $8^2 = 64$.

The next three numbers are $36, 49, 64$.

content.title: Finding patterns in geometry
content.problem:
Draw several triangles and measure the sum of their angles. What do you 
observe?
content.solution:
Triangle 1: $60° + 60° + 60° = 180°$

Triangle 2: $90° + 45° + 45° = 180°$

Triangle 3: $120° + 30° + 30° = 180°$

**Conjecture:** The sum of the angle measures in any triangle is $180°$.

content.text:
knowledge. Geometry's deductive method 
was developed partly to overcome this limitation.}

Inductive reasoning is powerful for discovery but has a crucial limitation: 
it cannot prove a conjecture with certainty. No matter how many examples 
confirm a pattern, the next example might break it.

content.title: A false conjecture
content.problem:
Consider the formula $f(n) = n^2 - n + 41$. Evaluate it for several values:
$$
 f(1) &= 1 - 1 + 41 = 41 \quad \text{(prime)} \\
 f(2) &= 4 - 2 + 41 = 43 \quad \text{(prime)} \\
 f(3) &= 9 - 3 + 41 = 47 \quad \text{(prime)} \\
 f(4) &= 16 - 4 + 41 = 53 \quad \text{(prime)} \\
 &\vdots
$$

One might conjecture that $f(n)$ is always prime. But $f(41) = 41^2 - 41 + 41 = 41^2 = 1681$, which is not prime!

This shows that even many confirming examples do not prove a conjecture.

content.title: Counterexample
content.content:
A **counterexample** is a specific case that 
shows a conjecture is false. A single counterexample disproves a conjecture.

content.title: Finding a counterexample
content.problem: Disprove the conjecture: "All prime numbers are odd."
content.solution:
Counterexample: $2$ is prime but not odd.

Therefore, the conjecture is false.

content.title: Geometric counterexample
content.problem: Disprove: "If two angles are supplementary, then both are obtuse."
content.solution:
Counterexample: $90°$ and $90°$ are supplementary (sum to $180°$), but 
neither is obtuse.

Or: $60°$ and $120°$ are supplementary, but $60°$ is acute.

The conjecture is false.

marginNotes.text:
But ask the beasts, and they will teach 
you; the birds of the heavens, and they will tell you... Who among all 
these does not know that the hand of the Lord has done this?
